## [00:00 - 03:01]

Music Alright, so now we're working on the customer winback flow. This is a great flow for customers that were previously engaged and who have lapsed and since not been engaged. This is a great way to get them excited and interested in your brand again. So the great thing about email and a customer winback is that you can bring back customers who previously purchased from you but had lapsed. So for example, if you had a brick and mortar store, you'd have to be relying on this person to purchase from you again off their own instinct. So for example, if you sold watches in stores and you didn't have any online component and you didn't have an email list and this person bought a watch from you three months ago, there's no really easy way to get that person back in the store other than maybe run ads online or on TV and hope that they came back in with email and in particular the customer winback. This is really a direct way to tell your customers that you missed them and ask them to come back. So typically you can incentivize them through a discount and just kind of pull out their heartstrings letting them know what you've been up to. So the customer winback really is good for brands that sell products that can be purchased every month or every couple months. Who it's not good for is a brand like a mattress company. So for example, I know I've shown Casper a couple times with Casper, you probably only have to purchase a mattress every three to five years if not longer. So Casper, if anything, would probably have to send their winback for at least a couple years down the road. With other companies though, this simply happens around three months or four months if your product is something that people can use fairly frequently.
